pathophysiology_summary: Erythema multiforme is an immune-mediated reaction, most commonly triggered by herpes simplex virus (here a cold sore ~10 days earlier), producing symmetrical fixed typical target lesions (three concentric zones: dusky/blistered centre, pale oedematous ring, erythematous halo) on acral extensor surfaces with centripetal spread. EM minor (no or minimal mucosal involvement, no systemic toxicity) is benign and self-limiting over 1–3 weeks; treatment is reassurance and symptomatic care, with consideration of HSV suppression if recurrent. The must-not-miss diagnosis is Stevens-Johnson syndrome/TEN — a severe cutaneous adverse reaction usually drug-triggered with prominent painful mucosal erosions (mouth/eyes/genitals), fever, atypical (two-zone) targets and skin detachment (positive Nikolsky) — which is excluded here by the careful mucosal/systemic screen. Discriminators: fixed multi-zone targets distinguish EM from transient migratory urticarial wheals, and the generalised symmetrical HSV-triggered distribution distinguishes it from a solitary fixed drug eruption. The key skill is a thorough mucosal screen before reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: Fixed three-zone target lesions, acral onset with centripetal spread, preceding cold sore.
Any target/atypical eruption must be screened for mucosal involvement, fever and skin detachment.
Wheals are transient/migratory without fixed three-zone targets or central epidermal damage.
Usually solitary/localised and drug-triggered, not generalised symmetrical acral with an HSV trigger.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Screen mucosae/systemic features to exclude SJS/TEN BEFORE reassuring; recognise HSV-triggered EM minor as benign.

differential.evidence_basis: Typical targets, no mucosal involvement/fever/sloughing, HSV trigger — benign EM minor.
No mucosal erosions, no fever, no skin detachment/Nikolsky — SJS/TEN excluded (remains a safety-net).
Working diagnosis: reassurance and symptomatic care, HSV suppression if recurrent, with a clear SJS/TEN safety-net.

red_flags.description: SJS/TEN features: painful mucosal (mouth/eye/genital) erosions, high fever, atypical targets, or skin detachment/positive Nikolsky
red_flags.clinical_significance: SJS/TEN is a life-threatening severe cutaneous adverse reaction needing emergency admission; must be excluded before reassurance.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed early SJS/TEN can progress to extensive skin/mucosal loss and death.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Calling it 'just a reaction' without screening mouth/eyes/genitals, missing early SJS/TEN.
Sending a clearly benign EM minor to ED, causing unnecessary alarm/resource use.
Not identifying the preceding cold sore (relevant for recurrence/suppression).

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: After a careful mucosal/systemic self-report screen, reassure and give symptomatic care with an explicit SJS/TEN safety-net; ED urgently if any mucosal involvement, fever or skin peeling develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring without a mucosal screen (missing SJS/TEN), or over-escalating a benign EM minor.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Erythema multiforme minor is usually HSV-associated and self-limiting; manage symptomatically and consider antiviral suppression for recurrent HSV-associated EM; exclude SJS/TEN by assessing mucosae and systemic features.
